relevant_files.rationale: Claude Code bootstrap template: the primary rule-injection file placed at .fabric/bootstrap/README.md. Its content defines Fabric's entire rule bootstrapping contract for the highest-capability client (hook + skill support). Only 6 lines — extreme brevity is a deliberate design choice for probabilistic-compliance contexts.
relevant_files.topic_relation: Directly represents how Fabric seeds the AI client's initial cognition via CLAUDE.md's @AGENTS.md include directive, which is Claude Code's native include mechanism.

key_code.description: 6-line file: announces README.md convergence, mandates fab_get_rules before any file edit, uses @AGENTS.md include — relies on Claude Code's native file-include protocol to delegate to the project-level AGENTS.md

relevant_files.rationale: The only template that uses a client-native format (.mdc with YAML frontmatter, alwaysApply: true). Contains a superset of CLAUDE.md content including the two extra rules about fab_update_registry and @HUMAN sections. This is the richest bootstrap template in the set.
relevant_files.topic_relation: Demonstrates how Cursor's alwaysApply:true activation mode differs from Claude's @include delegation — Cursor embeds rules directly in the .mdc payload rather than deferring to AGENTS.md lookup.

key_code.description: Cursor-native activation trigger: forces rule injection on every context window regardless of file path. No glob pattern specified — broadest possible activation scope.
Registry mutation guard: present in .mdc and roo/windsurf templates but absent from CLAUDE.md and GEMINI.md — an inconsistency that reduces rule coverage for Claude/Gemini clients.

relevant_files.rationale: Gemini CLI bootstrap template — byte-for-byte identical to CLAUDE.md. This is architecturally correct since both clients share the same file-include mechanism (@AGENTS.md), but it creates a maintenance surface: any rule evolution must be synchronized across two identical files.
relevant_files.topic_relation: Shows that Fabric treats Claude and Gemini as the same rule-loading tier despite them having different capability matrices (Claude has hook+skill, Gemini does not) — rule content is not yet differentiated by capability level.

key_code.description: Identical to CLAUDE.md. Gemini CLI reads GEMINI.md from project root as its native instruction file — Fabric's installation places this file there via GeminiCLIWriter.

relevant_files.rationale: Codex AGENTS.md header — the minimal 3-line variant, deliberately stripped of the fab_update_registry and @HUMAN rules. The comment 'this header is only for bootstrapping Codex to the Fabric project-level rules entry' acknowledges that Codex's AGENTS.md serves a different role (header prepend) than CLAUDE.md (standalone config file).
relevant_files.topic_relation: Reveals a structural gap: Codex receives fewer behavioral constraints in its bootstrap header compared to cursor/roo/windsurf clients, despite all using MCP. The missing rules are not loaded via any other mechanism.

key_code.description: Explicit comment that this file is a header, not a standalone rules file — implies Codex's AGENTS.md would be prepended to an existing file, not written wholesale.

relevant_files.rationale: Roo Code bootstrap template — same 7-line content as windsurf-fabric.md and cursor-fabric-bootstrap.mdc (minus YAML frontmatter). Roo uses .roo/rules/ directory with .md files, meaning this content is written there without any activation-mode metadata.
relevant_files.topic_relation: Roo Code's rule activation is always-on for files in .roo/rules/ — so the lack of YAML frontmatter is correct for Roo but means Fabric cannot leverage Roo's glob-scoped rules for more granular activation.

key_code.description: Full 5-rule set: project uses Fabric Protocol, fab_get_rules before edit, fab_update_registry for new nodes, @HUMAN stop for protected sections, fab_append_intent after task. Most complete rule set in the non-cursor templates.

relevant_files.rationale: Windsurf bootstrap template — byte-for-byte identical to roo-fabric.md. Windsurf supports 4 activation triggers (always_on, model_decision, glob, manual) with token caps, but Fabric writes this as a plain .md without any activation metadata, defaulting to whatever Windsurf's implicit behavior is.
relevant_files.topic_relation: Represents a missed optimization: Windsurf's model_decision trigger could be used for selective rule injection (e.g., only inject fab_get_rules context when editing code files), but Fabric doesn't leverage it — treating Windsurf identically to Roo despite different platform capabilities.

key_code.description: No activation metadata despite Windsurf supporting always_on/model_decision/glob/manual triggers. Fabric defaults to platform-default injection without specifying activation scope.

relevant_files.rationale: The forensic scanning engine: produces the project evidence package that seeds the 3-phase initialization interview. Uses text-pattern matching (not AST) across up to 5 entry-point files, 30 lines each, to infer framework, dominant patterns, and invariant proposals.
relevant_files.topic_relation: Defines the quality ceiling of automated pre-interview knowledge: HIGH confidence requires AST-level detection (only possible for ccclass decorators) or 80%+ sample coverage + 2+ co-occurring patterns. Most web framework detections will be MEDIUM at best.

key_code.description: Hard budget caps: only 5 entry files, first 30 lines each. For large projects this means forensic scanning captures <1% of codebase — sufficient for framework detection but inadequate for domain-specific invariant discovery.

Confidence algorithm: astLevel→HIGH, ratio<0.5→LOW, ratio>=0.8 && coOccurring.length>=2→HIGH, else→MEDIUM. React/Next/Vite patterns are text-only (astLevel=false), so they top out at MEDIUM even with strong evidence.

Only Cocos Creator achieves AST-level detection via @ccclass() decorator pattern — gives HIGH confidence. React/Next/Vite are text-pattern only, capping at MEDIUM confidence. This asymmetry means the forensic pipeline is significantly more reliable for Cocos projects.

Transitional deprecated function still consumed by SKILL.md Phase 2 via recommendations_for_skill field. The deprecation comment says to prefer assertions[], but SKILL.md still reads the old field — a live coupling that should be addressed.

relevant_files.rationale: The 3-phase initialization interview definition. Phase 1 confirms framework assumptions from forensic.json. Phase 2 extracts invariants (ban/require/protect) from recommendations_for_skill. Phase 3 writes init-context.json and generates layered AGENTS.md. Hard rules include 300-line root AGENTS.md limit and zero TODOs.
relevant_files.topic_relation: The interview protocol compensates for forensic.ts's limited scanning depth: what automated pattern matching can't determine (invariants, human-protected areas, architecture decisions) is extracted through structured Q&A. The 3-phase structure maps precisely to the AI client best practice of 'confirm → constrain → codify'.

key_code.description: Tripartite invariant taxonomy: ban (prohibited patterns), require (mandatory patterns), protect (human-owned areas). Maps directly to InitContext.invariants[].type enum. This taxonomy aligns with OWASP-style constraint enumeration — a domain best practice.
300-line cap on root AGENTS.md is a token-budget constraint — Claude Code's CLAUDE.md has diminishing compliance returns beyond ~500 tokens. The 300-line cap is conservative and domain-appropriate.
Mandatory precondition check before any interview step. If forensic.json is absent, skill terminates. This makes the pipeline strictly sequential: fab init → forensic.json → skill invocation.

relevant_files.rationale: Claude Code Stop Hook that enforces the pending-initialization state: if forensic.json exists but init-context.json doesn't, it emits a blocking signal. This is the only enforcement mechanism in the entire pipeline — all other clients lack this hook capability.
relevant_files.topic_relation: Demonstrates the capability gap between Claude Code CLI (hook+skill) and all other 6 clients (bootstrap+mcp only). For non-Claude clients, there is no mechanism to detect or enforce the initialization-pending state.

key_code.description: Exit-0 if forensic.json is absent (init not yet run) OR if init-context.json already exists (already initialized). Only blocks in the narrow window between fab init and completed interview — precise scope control.

Block signal with Chinese-language reason including skill invocation instructions and file references. The reason text is prescriptive: tells the AI exactly what to do next (invoke agents-md-init skill).

relevant_files.rationale: The rule resolution engine: accepts a file path, runs minimatch glob matching against all agents.meta.json nodes, sorts by priority (high→medium→low), partitions into L1/L2, and returns with L0 content always included. Also performs stale detection via client_hash comparison.
relevant_files.topic_relation: Core of Fabric's perception-phase rule delivery. The glob-only activation model here contrasts with Windsurf's model_decision trigger and Claude's path-scoped .claude/rules/ — Fabric centralizes all activation logic server-side via minimatch, making it client-agnostic but less capable than native client mechanisms.

key_code.description: Sole activation mechanism: every node in agents.meta.json has a scope_glob, and the requested path is matched against all globs via minimatch. No model_decision, no context signals — pure path-based routing. This is reliable but doesn't support context-sensitive rule injection.

Three-tier priority system with alphabetical tiebreaking on nodeId. Priority is statically declared on each node — there's no dynamic priority adjustment based on request context, which limits the engine's expressiveness.

Client sends previous revision_hash; if it doesn't match current meta.revision, stale=true is returned. This is advisory only — the current rules are always returned regardless of stale status. Clients must decide how to act on stale signals.

Context cache prevents re-reading agents.meta.json and README.md on every request. Cache is keyed by projectRoot. No TTL specified — cache persists for process lifetime, meaning rule changes require process restart unless chokidar invalidation is wired upstream.

relevant_files.rationale: Defines the AgentsMeta schema and auto-derivation logic for layer (L0/L1/L2) and topology_type (mirror/cross-cutting). The deriveAgentsMetaLayer() function implements depth-based layer assignment — directory depth 0=L0, 1-2=L1, 3+=L2.
relevant_files.topic_relation: The layer derivation algorithm is a key architectural decision: layers are structural (path-depth-based) rather than semantic (content-based). This aligns with Shadow Mirroring principle but means a cross-cutting concern at depth 1 would incorrectly get L1 classification unless it contains a _cross/ segment.

key_code.description: Layer derivation: depth 0 = L0, depth 1-2 = L1, depth 3+ = L2. Special case: _cross/ path segment forces L1 regardless of depth. AGENTS.md and .fabric/bootstrap/README.md are hardcoded as L0.

hasCrossCuttingSegment() checks for _cross/ in any path segment — enables cross-cutting concerns to override depth-based L1 classification. This is the only escape hatch from purely structural layer derivation.

relevant_files.rationale: InitContext schema — the data structure that persists 3-phase interview results. Contains framework, architecture_patterns, invariants (with ban/require/protect types and optional confidence_snapshot), domain_groups, and a full interview_trail for auditability.
relevant_files.topic_relation: The confidence_snapshot field on invariants is notable: it preserves the forensic assertion confidence level (HIGH/MEDIUM/LOW) and evidence references at the time the invariant was confirmed. This enables future rule quality analysis — e.g., identifying which invariants were confirmed with LOW confidence.

key_code.description: Optional confidence snapshot with evidence_refs array. This is a domain best practice: preserving provenance allows future tooling to distinguish user-confirmed HIGH-confidence invariants from auto-inferred LOW-confidence ones.

Full interview audit trail with phase, question, answer, presentation, and user_corrections. user_corrections field captures cases where AI presented incorrect information that the user corrected — important for calibrating future forensic scanner accuracy.

relevant_files.rationale: ForensicReport schema — the output contract for the forensic scanner. Defines 8 main sections: topology, framework, entry_points, code_samples, assertions, candidate_files, sampling_budget (fixed: 15 files / 100 lines), and recommendations_for_skill (deprecated).
relevant_files.topic_relation: The fixed sampling_budget (max_files: 15, max_lines_per_file: 100 as literal types in Zod) is a hard constraint that caps forensic quality. Industry equivalents like GitHub Copilot's workspace indexing use AST graphs across the entire codebase — Fabric's approach is intentionally lightweight but trades depth for speed.

key_code.description: Literal type constraints baked into the schema — these are not configurable defaults but schema-enforced constants. Any scanner output exceeding these values would fail Zod validation.
Deprecated field still present for backward compatibility with SKILL.md. The schema keeps it as optional to allow gradual migration to assertions[] — a pragmatic backward-compatibility decision.

relevant_files.rationale: Client capability matrix: maps 7 AI clients to their bootstrap/mcp/hook/skill capabilities. Only ClaudeCodeCLI has all 4 capabilities. All others have bootstrap+mcp only. Hook and skill capabilities are Claude-specific features.
relevant_files.topic_relation: The capability matrix directly determines rule enforcement quality. For Claude Code CLI, Fabric can enforce initialization via hooks and guide it via skills. For all other clients, enforcement relies entirely on LLM compliance with bootstrap text instructions — which external research confirms is probabilistic, not guaranteed.

key_code.description: Only client with hook:true and skill:true. This creates a two-tier reality: Claude users get enforced initialization pipeline, all other clients get best-effort rule injection with no enforcement.

Gemini is detected either by global ~/.gemini directory or by presence of a GEMINI.md in the project root — an unusual detection heuristic that could trigger false positives in projects that happen to have a GEMINI.md file for other reasons.

key_findings.title: Cross-Client Rule Content Fragmentation
key_findings.finding: The 6 bootstrap templates split into two distinct rule sets: (A) CLAUDE.md + GEMINI.md: 4 rules — no fab_update_registry, no @HUMAN stop; (B) cursor-fabric-bootstrap.mdc + roo-fabric.md + windsurf-fabric.md: 5 rules — includes fab_update_registry and @HUMAN stop. Codex gets only 3 rules. This creates inconsistent behavioral expectations across clients for the same Fabric operations.
key_findings.evidence: CLAUDE.md L1-L6 vs cursor-fabric-bootstrap.mdc L1-L11: Cursor gets 2 additional rules (fab_update_registry guard, @HUMAN section handling) that Claude/Gemini clients do not receive.
key_findings.recommendation: Canonicalize a single rule set in a shared source template. Use client-specific wrappers only for format differences (YAML frontmatter for Cursor, TOML for Codex). The 5-rule set from cursor is the most complete and should be the baseline.

key_findings.title: Windsurf Activation Model Underutilized
key_findings.finding: Windsurf supports 4 activation triggers (always_on, model_decision, glob, manual) with per-rule token caps. Fabric writes windsurf-fabric.md as plain Markdown without any activation metadata, defaulting to Windsurf's implicit behavior. This misses the opportunity to use model_decision for context-sensitive fab_get_rules injection or glob patterns to limit rule scope to code-editing contexts.
key_findings.evidence: windsurf-fabric.md L1-L7: plain Markdown, no Windsurf-specific frontmatter. Research context confirms Windsurf uses YAML frontmatter for trigger specification.
key_findings.recommendation: Add Windsurf-specific frontmatter with activation: always_on for the core fab_get_rules rule, and model_decision for the fab_update_registry and fab_append_intent rules to reduce unnecessary token consumption.

key_findings.title: Forensic Scanner Limited to Text Pattern Matching
key_findings.finding: The forensic scanner achieves AST-level confidence only for Cocos Creator projects (via @ccclass decorator detection). React, Next.js, and Vite pattern detection is pure text substring matching on up to 5 files × 30 lines = 150 lines maximum. The confidence algorithm caps at MEDIUM for text-only detections. This means the forensic evidence package for web projects is systematically lower quality than for Cocos projects.
key_findings.evidence: forensic.ts L85-L86: SAMPLE_LIMIT=5, SAMPLE_LINE_LIMIT=30. L298-L324: cocosCoOccurring → astLevel=true. L326-L349: reactCoOccurring → astLevel=false. L903-L926: determineConfidence() — astLevel required for HIGH.
key_findings.recommendation: For web frameworks, add import graph analysis (count unique react/next/vite imports across topology.files) as a proxy for AST-level confidence. This would elevate React/Next/Vite detections to HIGH without requiring a full AST parser.

key_findings.title: Single-Client Enforcement Model — Only Claude Gets Hooks
key_findings.finding: The initialization enforcement mechanism (Stop Hook blocking agent actions until init-context.json exists) is exclusive to Claude Code CLI. The 6 other clients have no equivalent enforcement. For non-Claude users, there is no mechanism to prevent AI agents from operating without a completed initialization — the bootstrap text instructions are the only guidance and compliance is probabilistic.
key_findings.evidence: resolver.ts L116-L127: ClaudeCodeCLI is the only client with hook:true and skill:true. agents-md-init-reminder.cjs is the sole enforcement point.
key_findings.recommendation: For clients without hook support, consider a pre-task guard in the fab_get_rules MCP response: if init-context.json doesn't exist and forensic.json does, include a high-priority warning in the L0 content instructing the AI to initiate the interview before proceeding.

key_findings.title: AGENTS.md Standard Compliance — Correct Structure, Missing Metadata
key_findings.finding: Fabric's AGENTS.md implementation follows the emerging standard's core structure (hierarchical, glob-scoped, L0/L1/L2 layering). However, standard implementations typically include explicit tool-override sections and environment variable declarations at L0. Fabric's L0 (bootstrap/README.md) focuses on workflow rules but doesn't include the AGENTS.md standard's recommended 'tools', 'env', and 'allowed_commands' sections.
key_findings.evidence: SKILL.md L62-L68: Root AGENTS.md structure includes L0 AI Constraints, @HUMAN, L1 Candidate Notes — but no tools/env sections. Research context: AGENTS.md standard has 60K+ OSS project adoption.
key_findings.recommendation: Consider adding optional tools and env sections to the root AGENTS.md template for projects that need to constrain AI tool access or declare required environment variables. This would improve alignment with the evolving AGENTS.md standard.

key_findings.title: Rule Activation is Path-Only (No Context-Sensitive Injection)
key_findings.finding: get-rules.ts uses minimatch glob matching as the sole activation mechanism. Rules are served based on the requested file path alone — no request context (user intent, edit type, conversation history) influences which rules are returned. This contrasts with Windsurf's model_decision trigger and Claude Code's native path-scoped .claude/rules/ which loads rules on-demand per sub-agent invocation.
key_findings.evidence: get-rules.ts L136-L161: loadRulesForPath() runs minimatch on all nodes, returns all matches by priority. No context signals beyond path.
key_findings.recommendation: Add an optional context field to GetRulesInput (e.g., operation: 'read'|'write'|'create'|'delete') and support operation-scoped scope_glob patterns in agents.meta.json nodes. This would allow high-token rules to be injected only for write operations.

key_findings.title: recommendations_for_skill Deprecation Not Complete
key_findings.finding: forensic.ts marks buildSkillRecommendations() as @deprecated and notes to prefer assertions[], but SKILL.md Phase 2 still reads recommendations_for_skill from forensic.json. The field is kept as optional in forensicReportSchema. This live coupling means the deprecation is incomplete — removing the field would break the interview skill.
key_findings.evidence: forensic.ts L1026-L1028: @deprecated comment. forensic-report.ts L89-L90: field kept as optional. SKILL.md L30-L31: 'Based on .fabric/forensic.json recommendations_for_skill list'.
key_findings.recommendation: Update SKILL.md Phase 2 to read from assertions[] instead of recommendations_for_skill. The assertions[] field contains structured confidence/evidence data that is strictly richer than the free-text recommendation strings.

key_findings.title: Context Cache Has No TTL — Rule Changes Require Process Restart
key_findings.finding: get-rules.ts contextCache caches the GetRulesContext (agents.meta.json + README.md content + human-lock) indefinitely. There is no TTL or explicit invalidation in the getRules service itself. While chokidar watches for changes in http.ts, the stdio transport (used by local MCP clients) has no such invalidation — rule changes during a session may not be reflected.
key_findings.evidence: get-rules.ts L79-L98: contextCache.get/set with no TTL parameter. L85: reads agents.meta.json and README.md once, caches indefinitely.
key_findings.recommendation: Add explicit cache invalidation in the getRules service: either a configurable TTL (e.g., 30s) or a pub/sub mechanism where fab_update_registry calls invalidate the context cache after writing new meta.

code_anchors.snippet:
---
alwaysApply: true
description: Fabric Protocol bootstrap rules
---

# Fabric Bootstrap
- 本项目使用 Fabric Protocol 管理规则。
- **任何文件修改前**，必须调 MCP tool `fab_get_rules(path=<被改文件>)` 获取规则。
- 新建 L1/L2 节点时，必须调 `fab_update_registry`，**严禁**直接编辑 `.fabric/agents.meta.json`。
- 涉及 @HUMAN 段（`.fabric/human-lock.json` 中列出）时，必须停下来请示人类。
- 每次完整任务结束，调 `fab_append_intent` 写一条意图记录。
code_anchors.significance: The only template using client-native format (YAML frontmatter + alwaysApply:true). Contains the most complete 5-rule set. All other templates should converge to this rule content. The alwaysApply ensures rules are always present in Cursor's context window — equivalent to Claude Code's Always rule tier.

code_anchors.snippet:
# Fabric Bootstrap
- 主说明文档已收敛到 `.fabric/bootstrap/README.md`。
- 项目级 bootstrap 入口仍然是 `AGENTS.md`。
- 修改任何文件前必须调用 `fab_get_rules(path=<被改文件>)`。

@AGENTS.md
code_anchors.significance: Only 3 behavioral rules (vs cursor's 5). Missing fab_update_registry guard and @HUMAN stop instruction. The @AGENTS.md delegation at line 6 is Claude Code's native include mechanism — it loads AGENTS.md content into the context window. This means Claude reads both CLAUDE.md AND AGENTS.md, while Cursor only reads the .mdc file.

The scanning budget constants define the quality ceiling: 5 files × 30 lines for pattern analysis, up to 12 candidate files total. These are hardcoded constants, not configuration — reflecting a deliberate choice to keep forensic scanning fast and predictable at the cost of depth.

Confidence algorithm: AST-level detection is the only reliable path to HIGH confidence for pattern assertions. React/Next/Vite patterns are text-only (astLevel=false), so they require 80%+ sample coverage AND 2+ co-occurring patterns to reach HIGH — a bar that may be difficult for web projects with diverse entry patterns.

Glob-only rule activation: every agent node is matched against the requested path via minimatch. No context signals, no model_decision equivalent. This is simpler than Windsurf/Cursor's native mechanisms but less expressive — all rules for a given path are always loaded, regardless of the operation type.

The enforcement gate: blocks precisely in the window between forensic.json creation and init-context.json creation. Exit-0 in all other states means zero overhead for initialized projects. The decision:block + structured JSON output follows Claude Code's Stop Hook API contract exactly.

Layer derivation is path-structural, not semantic. The _cross/ segment is the only semantic override. This means a deeply-nested file can accidentally get L2 classification if it's placed at depth 3+ in the .fabric/agents/ tree, even if it's intended as a cross-cutting L1 concern.

Claude Code CLI is the only fully-capable client. The capability matrix is binary per-feature (true/false) with no gradation — this means partial hook support (e.g., Gemini's planned hook API) cannot be expressed without schema changes.

call_chains.chain: init.ts → buildForensicReport() → forensic.ts (scan topology, detect framework, infer patterns) → buildFabricBootstrapGuide() → bootstrap-guide.ts (write .fabric/bootstrap/README.md) → resolveClients() → resolver.ts (detect 7 clients) → writers (inject MCP config per client) → install Stop Hook for Claude Code CLI → write forensic.json

call_chains.entry: Claude Code session start (post fab init, pre interview)
call_chains.chain: Stop Hook triggered → agents-md-init-reminder.cjs checks forensic.json ∧ ¬init-context.json → decision:block emitted → Claude Code blocks task → user invokes agents-md-init skill → SKILL.md Phase 1 (reads forensic.json framework/topology) → Phase 2 (reads recommendations_for_skill) → Phase 3 (writes init-context.json, generates layered AGENTS.md, updates agents.meta.json)

call_chains.entry: AI client requests rules before file edit (fab_get_rules MCP call)
call_chains.chain: AI calls fab_get_rules(path) → MCP server → get-rules.ts getRules() → loadGetRulesContext() (cache hit or readAgentsMeta + readFile README.md + readHumanLock) → resolveRulesForPath() → loadRulesForPath() (minimatch all nodes against path) → partitionRulesByLevel() (L1/L2 split) → return {L0, L1[], L2[], human_locked_nearby[]} → appendGetRulesAuditEvent() (best-effort telemetry)

call_chains.entry: Cursor rule injection (no hooks, bootstrap-only path)
call_chains.chain: cursor-fabric-bootstrap.mdc loaded by Cursor at session start (alwaysApply:true) → AI reads 5 rules → AI decides to edit file → rule text instructs fab_get_rules call → AI calls fab_get_rules via MCP → same get-rules.ts path as Claude → rules returned → AI proceeds with context

questions_for_user: The rule content split between CLAUDE.md (3 rules) and cursor/roo/windsurf (5 rules) appears unintentional — was the omission of fab_update_registry and @HUMAN rules from CLAUDE.md a deliberate decision (Claude has hook enforcement so text rules are less critical) or an oversight?

For non-Claude clients (Cursor, Windsurf, Roo, Gemini, Codex) that lack hook enforcement, is there a plan to add an initialization guard in the fab_get_rules MCP response when init-context.json is absent? Or is the assumption that non-Claude users will manually run the interview?

The forensic scanner's fixed budget (5 files × 30 lines) was presumably chosen for speed. Have you measured how often this budget is insufficient for real projects — e.g., does a React project with 200+ components get meaningfully worse forensic output than a Cocos project with 20 scripts?

Windsurf's model_decision activation trigger could allow selective rule injection (e.g., only inject registry rules during write operations). Is there a plan to add platform-specific activation metadata to windsurf-fabric.md, or is the current always-on approach preferred for simplicity?

The contextCache in get-rules.ts has no TTL, meaning rule changes during a stdio MCP session (e.g., after running fab_update_registry) would not be reflected until process restart. Is this a known limitation or is there invalidation happening upstream that compensates?

The recommendations_for_skill field is marked deprecated in favor of assertions[], but SKILL.md still reads it. What is the migration timeline? Is there a concern that assertions[] doesn't surface the same actionable interview questions that the free-text recommendations did?

_metadata.perspective: Domain Expert - AI 客户端领域最佳实践与标准
_metadata.layer: Layer 2+3 — Structure Tracing + Code Anchor Extraction
